DOCTRINE_BASIC_STRUCTURE.label: Basic Structure
DOCTRINE_BASIC_STRUCTURE.establishing_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973), Minerva Mills v. UOI (1980), I.R. Coelho v. State of TN (2007)
requires: [AND]
Constitutional amendment under Article 368
Amendment "damages/emasculates/destroys/abrogates" basic features
defeaters: Amendment does not alter basic features
Incidental effect without substantial damage
Amendment pre-dates April 24, 1973 (per Waman Rao)
DOCTRINE_BASIC_STRUCTURE.key_phrases: "emasculate basic features", "destroy identity of Constitution", "beyond Parliament's constituent power"

DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_TEST.label: Proportionality Test
DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_TEST.establishing_cases: Modern Dental College v. State of MP (2016), K.S. Puttaswamy I (2017), K.S. Puttaswamy II/Aadhaar (2018)
requires: [AND - four prongs]
Legitimate aim (the measure pursues a lawful/legitimate objective)
Suitability / rational connection (the measure is capable of achieving that aim)
Necessity / least restrictive means (no equally effective but less restrictive alternative)
Balancing / proportionality stricto sensu (overall benefits outweigh the rights infringement; fair balance)
defeaters: No legitimate aim
Less restrictive alternatives available
Disproportionate impact on rights
No rational nexus
DOCTRINE_PROPORTIONALITY_TEST.key_phrases: "legitimate aim", "rational nexus", "least restrictive means", "proportionality stricto sensu"

DOCTRINE_MANIFEST_ARBITRARINESS.label: Manifest Arbitrariness
DOCTRINE_MANIFEST_ARBITRARINESS.establishing_cases: Shayara Bano v. UOI (2017), Navtej Singh Johar v. UOI (2018), Joseph Shine v. UOI (2018)
requires: [OR - any one suffices]
Legislation is "capricious"
Legislation is "irrational"
Legislation "without adequate determining principle"
Legislation is "excessive and disproportionate"
Non-classification: failure to recognize degrees of harm
defeaters: Rational basis and determining principle present
Policy decision within legislative wisdom
Public interest justification

DOCTRINE_MANEKA_GANDHI_FRAMEWORK_DUE_PRO.label: Maneka Gandhi Framework (Due Process)
DOCTRINE_MANEKA_GANDHI_FRAMEWORK_DUE_PRO.establishing_cases: Maneka Gandhi v. UOI (1978), R.C. Cooper v. UOI (1970)
requires: [AND - Golden Triangle test]
Procedure established by law exists
Procedure must be "right, just and fair"
Procedure must not be "arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive"
Must satisfy Article 14 (non-arbitrariness)
Must satisfy Article 19 (reasonableness if applicable)
Must conform to principles of natural justice
defeaters: Express statutory exclusion of natural justice
Emergency situations
Clear legislative intent excluding procedural requirements
DOCTRINE_MANEKA_GANDHI_FRAMEWORK_DUE_PRO.key_phrases: "just, fair and reasonable", "right and just and fair", "not arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive", "Golden Triangle"

DOCTRINE_REASONABLE_CLASSIFICATION_ARTIC.label: Reasonable Classification (Article 14 Twin Test)
DOCTRINE_REASONABLE_CLASSIFICATION_ARTIC.establishing_cases: State of WB v. Anwar Ali Sarkar (1952), Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Tendolkar (1958)
requires: [AND - twin test]
Intelligible differentia (a clear, non-arbitrary basis for classification)
Rational nexus (the differentia has a rational relation to the object sought to be achieved)
defeaters: No intelligible differentia (arbitrary grouping)
No rational nexus to legislative object
Over-inclusion/under-inclusion without justification
DOCTRINE_REASONABLE_CLASSIFICATION_ARTIC.typical_edge_pattern: (Classification) --[twin test]--> (Valid if both satisfied) OR (Violates Art14 if fails)

DOCTRINE_WEDNESBURY_UNREASONABLENESS.label: Wednesbury Unreasonableness
DOCTRINE_WEDNESBURY_UNREASONABLENESS.establishing_cases: Om Kumar v. UOI (2000), Tata Cellular v. UOI (1994)
requires: [OR - any one suffices]
Decision based on irrelevant considerations
Decision ignores relevant considerations
Decision "so outrageous that no sensible person could have arrived at it"
defeaters: Fundamental rights involved (proportionality applies instead)
Decision within reasonable range of outcomes
Policy matter within executive domain
DOCTRINE_WEDNESBURY_UNREASONABLENESS.key_phrases: "so unreasonable that no reasonable authority", "outrageous in defiance of logic", "secondary review"

DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_EXPECTATION.label: Legitimate Expectation
DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_EXPECTATION.establishing_cases: State of Kerala v. K.G. Madhavan Pillai (1988), Food Corporation v. Kamdhenu (1993)
requires: [OR - source of expectation]
Express promise or assurance by public authority
Consistent past practice/established pattern
Representation creating reasonable expectation
defeaters: Overriding public interest
Change in statutory provisions
Misrepresentation by claimant
Expectation contrary to law
DOCTRINE_LEGITIMATE_EXPECTATION.key_phrases: "reasonable expectation", "consistent past practice", "express promise", "fair play in action"

DOCTRINE_NATURAL_JUSTICE_AUDI_ALTERAM_PA.label: Natural Justice - Audi Alteram Partem
DOCTRINE_NATURAL_JUSTICE_AUDI_ALTERAM_PA.establishing_cases: Maneka Gandhi v. UOI (1978), A.K. Kraipak v. UOI (1969)
requires: [AND]
Notice of proposed action
Reasonable opportunity to be heard
Fair hearing before adverse decision
Speaking order/reasoned decision
defeaters: Urgency/emergency (post-decision hearing may suffice)
Statutory exclusion (subject to Art 14 challenge)
Purely administrative (non-quasi-judicial) actions
Conclusion obvious and hearing could not make difference
DOCTRINE_NATURAL_JUSTICE_AUDI_ALTERAM_PA.key_phrases: "no one shall be condemned unheard", "fair opportunity to answer", "speaking order"

DOCTRINE_HARMONIOUS_CONSTRUCTION.label: Harmonious Construction
DOCTRINE_HARMONIOUS_CONSTRUCTION.establishing_cases: CIT v. Hindustan Bulk Carriers (2003) - five principles
requires: [AND - five principles]
Avoid a head‑on clash between provisions (prefer a reading that reconciles them)
Construe provisions harmoniously so each can operate in its proper sphere
One provision should not defeat another unless they are truly irreconcilable
Give effect to both provisions as far as possible (prefer coexistence over nullification)
Avoid a construction that renders any provision redundant, otiose, or dead letter
defeaters: Irreconcilable conflict
Clear legislative intent for one to prevail
Later enactment expressly overrides earlier
DOCTRINE_HARMONIOUS_CONSTRUCTION.key_phrases: "avoid head-on clash", "construe harmoniously", "neither provision rendered otiose"

DOCTRINE_PITH_AND_SUBSTANCE.label: Pith and Substance
DOCTRINE_PITH_AND_SUBSTANCE.establishing_cases: State of Bombay v. F.N. Balsara (1951), Kartar Singh v. State of Punjab (1994)
requires: [AND]
Challenge to legislative competence based on List entries
Court determines "true nature and character" of legislation
Dominant character must fall within enacting body's list
defeaters: Legislation in pith and substance belongs to another list
Cannot validate legislation if substance clearly outside jurisdiction
DOCTRINE_PITH_AND_SUBSTANCE.typical_edge_pattern: (Legislation) --[pith and substance]--> (Valid if dominant character in correct list)

DOCTRINE_READING_DOWN.label: Reading Down
DOCTRINE_READING_DOWN.establishing_cases: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar (1962), State of Bombay v. FN Balsara (1951)
requires: [AND]
Provision capable of two interpretations
One interpretation constitutional, other unconstitutional
Reading down possible without rewriting
Legislative intent not frustrated by narrower reading
defeaters: Only one reasonable interpretation possible
Reading down would rewrite statute
Core of provision is unconstitutional
DOCTRINE_READING_DOWN.key_phrases: "read down to save constitutionality", "presumption of constitutionality"

CONCEPT_MURDER.label: Murder
requires: [AND]
Actus reus: act causing death of a human being

Mens rea: intention to cause death (IPC 300 firstly / BNS 101 firstly) OR intention to cause bodily injury known to be likely to cause death (IPC 300 secondly / BNS 101 secondly) OR intention to cause bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death (IPC 300 thirdly / BNS 101 thirdly) OR knowledge that the act is so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death (IPC 300 fourthly / BNS 101 fourthly)
Death actually results from the act
The act constitutes culpable homicide amounting to murder (i.e., does not fall within any of the five exceptions to IPC 300 / BNS 101)
defeaters: Right of private defence (IPC 96-106 / BNS 34-44)
Insanity / unsoundness of mind (IPC 84 / BNS 22)
Accident without criminal intent or knowledge (IPC 80 / BNS 20)
Act of a child under 7 years (IPC 82 / BNS 17) or under 12 with immature understanding (IPC 83 / BNS 18)
Sudden and grave provocation — Exception 1 to IPC 300 / BNS 101 (reduces to culpable homicide not amounting to murder)
Right of private defence exceeded in good faith — Exception 2 to IPC 300 / BNS 101
Public servant acting in good faith for public justice — Exception 3 to IPC 300 / BNS 101
Sudden fight in heat of passion without premeditation — Exception 4 to IPC 300 / BNS 101
Consent of victim above 18 years — Exception 5 to IPC 300 / BNS 101
CONCEPT_MURDER.key_phrases: "committed murder", "Section 302", "BNS 103", "intention to kill", "culpable homicide amounting to murder", "murdered the deceased", "bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death", "imminently dangerous act"
CONCEPT_MURDER.establishing_cases: Virsa Singh v. State of Punjab (1958), Rajwant Singh v. State of Kerala (1966), State of AP v. R. Punnayya (1977), Maqsoodan v. State of UP (1983)

CONCEPT_CULPABLE_HOMICIDE.label: Culpable Homicide
requires: [AND]
Actus reus: act causing death of a human being

Mens rea (any one): intention of causing death (IPC 299 firstly / BNS 100 firstly) OR intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death (IPC 299 secondly / BNS 100 secondly) OR knowledge that the act is likely to cause death (IPC 299 thirdly / BNS 100 thirdly)
Death actually results
The act falls within one of the five exceptions to IPC 300 / BNS 101 (reducing it from murder) OR the mens rea does not reach the threshold for murder
defeaters: Right of private defence (IPC 96-106 / BNS 34-44)
Insanity / unsoundness of mind (IPC 84 / BNS 22)
Accident without criminal intent or knowledge (IPC 80 / BNS 20)
Act of a child under 7 (IPC 82 / BNS 17)
Act done under duress / coercion (IPC 94 / BNS 32)
No intention or knowledge — death purely accidental and not negligent
CONCEPT_CULPABLE_HOMICIDE.key_phrases: "culpable homicide not amounting to murder", "Section 304", "BNS 105", "Part I", "Part II", "knowledge that act likely to cause death", "intention to cause bodily injury likely to cause death", "Exception 1 to Section 300"
CONCEPT_CULPABLE_HOMICIDE.establishing_cases: State of AP v. R. Punnayya (1977), K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1962), Pulicherla Nagaraju v. State of AP (2006), Smt. Jasvinder Kaur v. State of Punjab (2023)

CONCEPT_DEATH_BY_NEGLIGENCE.label: Death By Negligence
requires: [AND]
Death of a person is caused
Death is caused by a rash or negligent act of the accused
No intention to cause death or knowledge that the act is likely to cause death
The act amounts to criminal rashness or criminal negligence (not mere civil negligence)
defeaters: Act was not rash or negligent — it was a pure accident with no fault
Reasonable care was exercised (standard of care satisfied)
Contributory negligence of the victim (mitigating but not always a complete defense)
Act done under necessity or emergency
For medical practitioners under BNS 106(2): registered medical practitioner performing medical procedure — gross negligence must be shown (not simple negligence)
CONCEPT_DEATH_BY_NEGLIGENCE.key_phrases: "death by negligence", "Section 304A", "BNS 106", "rash or negligent act", "criminal negligence", "death caused by rash and negligent act", "gross negligence", "medical negligence"
CONCEPT_DEATH_BY_NEGLIGENCE.establishing_cases: Jacob Mathew v. State of Punjab (2005), Empress of India v. Idu Beg (1881), Juggankhan v. State of MP (1965), Alister Anthony Pareira v. State of Maharashtra (2012)

CONCEPT_DOWRY_DEATH.label: Dowry Death
requires: [AND]
Death of a woman
Death occurs otherwise than under normal circumstances
Death occurs within seven years of marriage
It is shown that soon before her death she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or any relative of the husband
Such cruelty or harassment was in connection with demand for dowry
defeaters: Death occurred under normal circumstances (natural death / disease)
Death occurred beyond seven years of marriage (statutory presumption under IPC 113B IEA / BSA 118 does not apply, though general law may still apply)
No evidence of cruelty or harassment 'soon before death'
Cruelty/harassment was unrelated to dowry demand
Accused proves that no dowry demand was made
CONCEPT_DOWRY_DEATH.key_phrases: "dowry death", "Section 304B", "BNS 80", "soon before her death", "cruelty in connection with dowry demand", "death within seven years of marriage", "otherwise than under normal circumstances", "presumption as to dowry death"
CONCEPT_DOWRY_DEATH.establishing_cases: Shanti v. State of Haryana (1991), Kans Raj v. State of Punjab (2000), Pawan Kumar v. State of Haryana (1998), Satvir Singh v. State of Punjab (2001)

CONCEPT_ATTEMPT_MURDER.label: Attempt Murder
requires: [AND]
The accused did an act towards the commission of murder
The act was done with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if the accused had caused death, they would have been guilty of murder
The act went beyond mere preparation and constituted a direct move towards commission of the offense
Death did not result (if death results, the charge is murder, not attempt)
defeaters: Right of private defence (IPC 96-106 / BNS 34-44)
Insanity / unsoundness of mind (IPC 84 / BNS 22)
Lack of mens rea — no intention or knowledge requisite for murder
Act was merely preparatory and did not cross the threshold into attempt
Injuries are simple or trivial — may indicate lesser offense (IPC 324/326 / BNS 118/119) rather than attempt to murder
CONCEPT_ATTEMPT_MURDER.key_phrases: "attempt to murder", "Section 307", "BNS 109", "intended to commit murder", "with such intention or knowledge", "did an act towards the commission of murder", "if he by that act caused death would have been guilty of murder", "life imprisonment for the remainder of natural life"
CONCEPT_ATTEMPT_MURDER.establishing_cases: State of Maharashtra v. Balram Bama Patil (1983), Vasant Vithu Jadhav v. State of Maharashtra (2004), R. Prakash v. State of Karnataka (2004), Sarju Prasad v. State of Bihar (1965)

CONCEPT_RAPE_DEFINITION.label: Rape Definition
notes: **Age of consent for married woman 15→18 (implements Independent Thought v. UOI)**
requires: [OR — any clause of IPC 375 / BNS 63]
Penetration of penis into vagina, mouth, urethra, or anus of a woman, or making her do so with him or another person
Insertion of any object or body part (not penis) into vagina, urethra, or anus of a woman, or making her do so
Manipulation of any body part of a woman to cause penetration into her vagina, urethra, or anus, or making her do so
Application of mouth to the vagina, anus, or urethra of a woman, or making her do so

[AND with any one of the following circumstances]: Against her will / Without her consent / With consent obtained by fear of death or hurt / With consent under misconception of being lawfully married / Consent given while of unsound mind or intoxicated / With or without consent when under 18 years of age / When she is unable to communicate consent
defeaters: Valid free consent of the woman (above 18 years, not obtained by coercion, fear, misconception, or intoxication)
Medical procedure or intervention (not sexual in nature)
Accused proves mistaken belief of lawful marriage (extremely narrow — jurisprudentially contested)
Note: Marital rape exception for wife above 18 still exists under IPC 375 Exception 2 / BNS 63 Exception 2 but is under constitutional challenge
CONCEPT_RAPE_DEFINITION.key_phrases: "rape", "Section 375", "BNS 63", "without her consent", "against her will", "penetration", "sexual assault", "consent obtained by fear", "age of consent", "sexual intercourse"
CONCEPT_RAPE_DEFINITION.establishing_cases: Tukaram v. State of Maharashtra (Mathura case) (1979), State of Punjab v. Gurmit Singh (1996), Independent Thought v. Union of India (2017), State of Rajasthan v. Shambhu Kewat (2023)

CONCEPT_RAPE_PUNISHMENT.label: Rape Punishment
requires: [AND]
The offense of rape under IPC 375 / BNS 63 is established

Determination of applicable punishment category: simple rape (IPC 376(1) / BNS 64(1)) — minimum 10 years RI extendable to life; OR aggravated rape by specified persons (IPC 376(2) / BNS 64(2)) — minimum 10 years RI extendable to life imprisonment for remainder of natural life; OR rape of woman under 16 (IPC 376(3) / BNS 65(1)) — minimum 20 years extendable to life; OR rape of woman under 12 — minimum 20 years or life or death
Court determines appropriate sentence within statutory range
defeaters: Acquittal on charge of rape (offense not established)
Compromise between parties — NOT permissible in rape cases (Shimbhu v. State of Haryana, 2014)
Note: No defeater reduces mandatory minimum sentence — court must impose at least the statutory minimum unless recording special and adequate reasons under proviso
CONCEPT_RAPE_PUNISHMENT.key_phrases: "punishment for rape", "Section 376", "BNS 64", "rigorous imprisonment not less than ten years", "imprisonment for life", "aggravated rape", "remainder of natural life", "special and adequate reasons"
CONCEPT_RAPE_PUNISHMENT.establishing_cases: State of Rajasthan v. Shera Ram (2012), Shimbhu v. State of Haryana (2014), Mukesh v. State (NCT of Delhi) (Nirbhaya case) (2017), Nipun Saxena v. Union of India (2019)

CONCEPT_GANG_RAPE.label: Gang Rape
requires: [AND]
Rape as defined under IPC 375 / BNS 63 is committed
The rape is committed by one or more persons constituting a group, acting in furtherance of their common intention
Each person in the group is deemed to have committed rape (constructive liability)
The group consists of two or more persons (IPC 376D required 'one or more'; BNS 70(1) requires group action)
defeaters: Rape itself is not established
Accused was not part of the group or did not share common intention
Accused proves they were not present at the scene and had no prior concert
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), minority (IPC 82-83 / BNS 17-18)
CONCEPT_GANG_RAPE.typical_edge_pattern: (Group_of_Persons) --[common intention]--> (Rape_Committed) --[each person deemed to commit rape]--> (Conviction_IPC376D/BNS70(1))
CONCEPT_GANG_RAPE.key_phrases: "gang rape", "Section 376D", "BNS 70", "group of persons", "acting in furtherance of common intention", "each person deemed to have committed rape", "minimum twenty years", "imprisonment for remainder of natural life"
CONCEPT_GANG_RAPE.establishing_cases: Mukesh v. State (NCT of Delhi) (Nirbhaya case) (2017), Priya Patel v. State of MP (2006), State of Rajasthan v. Noore Khan (2018)

CONCEPT_SEXUAL_INTERCOURSE_BY_DECEIT.label: Sexual Intercourse By Deceit
notes: **NEW - false promise of marriage, up to 10 years**
requires: [AND]
Sexual intercourse with a woman
The intercourse is not constituting the offense of rape

Sexual intercourse is obtained by deceitful means: false promise of employment or promotion, or by inducement or by suppressing identity, including by impersonating, or by false promise of marriage with no intention of fulfilling the same
The promise or representation was made without genuine intention to honor it
defeaters: Promise of marriage was genuine at the time of making but failed due to subsequent circumstances (genuine breach vs. fraudulent inducement — Pramod Suryabhan Pawar v. State of Maharashtra, 2019)
No deceit — the woman had full knowledge and gave free voluntary consent
The act constitutes rape under BNS 63 (charge under BNS 69 is subsidiary)
General defenses: insanity (BNS 22), act of minor (BNS 17-18)
CONCEPT_SEXUAL_INTERCOURSE_BY_DECEIT.key_phrases: "sexual intercourse by deceit", "BNS 69", "false promise of marriage", "deceitful means", "suppressing identity", "impersonation", "inducement", "false promise of employment", "consent by deception"
CONCEPT_SEXUAL_INTERCOURSE_BY_DECEIT.establishing_cases: Pramod Suryabhan Pawar v. State of Maharashtra (2019), Deepak Gulati v. State of Haryana (2013), Anurag Soni v. State of Chhattisgarh (2019)

CONCEPT_THEFT.label: Theft
requires: [AND]
Moveable property involved
The property is taken out of the possession of another person without that person's consent
The taking is dishonest — the accused intends to cause wrongful gain to themselves or wrongful loss to the possessor (IPC 378 / BNS 303)
The accused moves the property in order to such taking
defeaters: Bona fide claim of right over the property (IPC 79 / BNS 19 — act done in good faith believing justified by law)
Consent of the person in possession
Property is immovable (theft applies only to moveable property)
Taking was not dishonest — no intention of wrongful gain or loss
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), minority (IPC 82-83 / BNS 17-18)
CONCEPT_THEFT.key_phrases: "theft", "Section 378", "Section 379", "BNS 303", "BNS 305", "dishonestly takes moveable property", "out of the possession of any person", "without consent", "moves that property", "intending to take dishonestly"
CONCEPT_THEFT.establishing_cases: K.N. Mehra v. State of Rajasthan (1957), Pyare Lal Bhargava v. State of Rajasthan (1963), Avtar Singh v. State of Punjab (1965)

CONCEPT_SNATCHING.label: Snatching
notes: **NEW offense - "suddenly or quickly or forcibly seizes" - up to 3 years**
requires: [AND]
The accused suddenly or quickly or forcibly seizes or secures moveable property from another person
The act of seizing or snatching is from the person or in the presence of the person
No weapon is used and no attempt to cause death, hurt, or wrongful restraint (otherwise it becomes robbery)
defeaters: Force used amounts to 'voluntarily causing hurt' — offense escalates to robbery (BNS 309)
Bona fide claim of right
Consent of the person from whom property is taken
General defenses: insanity (BNS 22), minority (BNS 17-18)
CONCEPT_SNATCHING.key_phrases: "snatching", "BNS 304", "suddenly or quickly or forcibly seizes", "secures moveable property", "snatched from the person", "up to three years imprisonment"
CONCEPT_SNATCHING.establishing_cases: Note: BNS 304 is a new provision with no pre-existing IPC equivalent; landmark cases are yet to develop. Courts may draw on robbery/theft jurisprudence for analogous principles.

CONCEPT_ROBBERY_DACOITY.label: Robbery/Dacoity
requires: [OR — Robbery or Dacoity]

ROBBERY (IPC 390 / BNS 309): [AND] Theft or extortion is committed + the offender voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, or wrongful restraint OR puts or attempts to put the person in fear of instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint to commit or carry away the stolen property or retain it
DACOITY (IPC 391 / BNS 310): [AND] Robbery is committed + five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit the robbery
defeaters: No theft or extortion was committed (foundational offense not established)
No use or threat of force (remains theft, not robbery)
Fewer than five persons involved (dacoity charge fails; may still be robbery)
Right of private defence (IPC 96-106 / BNS 34-44)
Bona fide claim of right over property
General defenses: insanity, minority
CONCEPT_ROBBERY_DACOITY.key_phrases: "robbery", "dacoity", "Section 390", "Section 391", "BNS 309", "BNS 310", "voluntarily causing hurt", "fear of instant death", "five or more persons conjointly", "gang robbery"
CONCEPT_ROBBERY_DACOITY.establishing_cases: Thakore Karansinhji Bhavansinhji v. State of Gujarat (1989), State of Maharashtra v. Joseph Mingel Koli (1997), Phool Kumar v. State of NCT of Delhi (2014)

CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_BREACH_TRUST.label: Criminal Breach Trust
requires: [AND]
The accused was entrusted with property OR had dominion over property
The accused dishonestly misappropriated or converted the property to their own use
OR the accused dishonestly used or disposed of the property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract made

The entrustment was in the capacity of: general person (IPC 405 / BNS 316(1)) OR carrier/warehouse-keeper/clerk/servant (IPC 406-408 / BNS 316(2)-(3)) OR public servant/banker/merchant/agent (IPC 409 / BNS 316(4))
defeaters: No entrustment of property — accused had independent possession/ownership
No dishonest intention — the conversion or use was bona fide
Property was used within the terms of the trust or contract
Civil dispute — mere breach of contract without dishonest intent does not constitute CBT
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), mistake of fact in good faith (IPC 76/79 / BNS 14/19)
CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_BREACH_TRUST.key_phrases: "criminal breach of trust", "Section 405", "Section 406", "Section 409", "BNS 316", "entrusted with property", "dishonestly misappropriated", "dominion over property", "in violation of legal contract", "converted to his own use"
CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_BREACH_TRUST.establishing_cases: Jaswantrai Manilal Akhaney v. State of Bombay (1956), R.K. Dalmia v. Delhi Administration (1962), Rashmi Kumar v. Mahesh Kumar Bhada (1997), Velji Raghavji Patel v. State of Maharashtra (1965)

CONCEPT_CHEATING.label: Cheating
requires: [AND]
The accused deceived another person (the victim)

The deception was practiced by: fraudulently or dishonestly inducing the deceived person to deliver property to any person, OR to consent to retain property, OR to intentionally induce the person deceived to do or omit to do anything which they would not do or omit if not so deceived (IPC 415 / BNS 318)
The deception caused damage or harm to the person deceived in body, mind, reputation, or property
For cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery (IPC 420 / BNS 318): the accused also induced the victim to deliver property or valuable security or to make, alter, or destroy a document
defeaters: No deception — the representation was true or believed to be true in good faith
No dishonest or fraudulent intention at inception (mere breach of promise vs. initial fraudulent intent)
Victim suffered no damage or harm
Civil dispute — mere failure to fulfill a promise without initial deception does not constitute cheating
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), mistake of fact (IPC 76/79 / BNS 14/19)
CONCEPT_CHEATING.key_phrases: "cheating", "Section 415", "Section 420", "BNS 318", "deceived", "fraudulently induced", "dishonestly induced delivery of property", "valuable security", "cheated and dishonestly induced"
CONCEPT_CHEATING.establishing_cases: Hridaya Ranjan Prasad Verma v. State of Bihar (2000), Vimla v. Delhi Administration (1963), Mahadeo Prasad v. State of WB (1954), S.W. Palanitkar v. State of Bihar (2002)

CONCEPT_SEDITION_TO_SOVEREIGNTY.label: Sedition→Sovereignty
notes: **IPC 124A ("sedition") replaced by BNS 152 ("acts endangering sovereignty, unity and integrity of India"). Punishment: imprisonment for life OR up to 7 years; also liable to fine. Text expressly references secession/armed rebellion/subversive activities/separatist feelings.**
requires: [AND — under BNS 152]
Acts or attempts to excite or attempts to excite secession or armed rebellion or subversive activities
OR encourages feelings of separatist activities endangering sovereignty or unity and integrity of India
OR engages in or commits any such act
Such act must endanger the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India

Note: Under old IPC 124A (sedition — now replaced): words or signs or visible representation brought or attempted to bring into hatred or contempt or excited or attempted to excite disaffection towards the Government established by law
defeaters: Expression of disapprobation of government measures/actions without exciting disaffection (IPC 124A Explanation 2-3)
Legitimate criticism of government policy without incitement to violence
Freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a) — act does not fall within reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2)
No tendency to incite violence or create public disorder (Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar, 1962)
Mere expression of opinion without incitement to armed rebellion, secession, or subversive activities (under BNS 152)
CONCEPT_SEDITION_TO_SOVEREIGNTY.key_phrases: "sedition", "Section 124A", "BNS 152", "sovereignty, unity and integrity", "acts endangering sovereignty", "secession", "armed rebellion", "subversive activities", "separatist feelings", "disaffection towards the Government"
CONCEPT_SEDITION_TO_SOVEREIGNTY.establishing_cases: Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar (1962), Balwant Singh v. State of Punjab (1995), S.G. Vombatkere v. Union of India (2022 — Supreme Court kept IPC 124A in abeyance pending review), Vinod Dua v. Union of India (2021)

CONCEPT_COMMON_INTENTION.label: Common Intention
requires: [AND]
A criminal act is committed by two or more persons
The criminal act is done in furtherance of the common intention of all

Pre-arranged plan or prior meeting of minds is not necessary — common intention can develop on the spot during the course of the transaction and can be inferred from the conduct of the accused and circumstances of the case (Pandurang v. State of Hyderabad, 1955)
Active participation — each person need not perform every part of the offense, but must participate in some manner in the criminal act
defeaters: No common intention — each person acted independently with separate intentions
Accused was merely present at the scene but did not share the common intention
Common intention was for a different offense than what was actually committed (unless the committed offense was a probable consequence)
Accused had withdrawn from the common design before commission of the offense
CONCEPT_COMMON_INTENTION.typical_edge_pattern: (Two_or_More_Persons) --[pre-arranged plan/common intention]--> (Criminal_Act_Committed) --[each liable for act done in furtherance]--> (Constructive_Liability_IPC34/BNS3(5))
CONCEPT_COMMON_INTENTION.key_phrases: "common intention", "Section 34", "BNS 3(5)", "in furtherance of common intention", "pre-arranged plan", "prior meeting of minds", "each of several persons", "constructive liability", "acted in concert"
CONCEPT_COMMON_INTENTION.establishing_cases: Mahbub Shah v. King Emperor (1945), Pandurang v. State of Hyderabad (1955), Surendra Chauhan v. State of MP (2000), Dukhmochan Pandey v. State of Bihar (1957)

CONCEPT_DEFAMATION.label: Defamation
requires: [AND]
The accused made or published an imputation concerning another person
The imputation was made by words (spoken or written), or by signs, or by visible representation
The imputation was intended to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that it would harm, the reputation of the person

The imputation lowered the moral or intellectual character, or character in respect of caste/calling, or the credit of that person, or caused it to be believed that the body of that person is in a loathsome state
defeaters: First Exception (Truth for public good): imputation is true and publication is for public good (IPC 499 Ex.1 / BNS 356)
Second Exception: public conduct of public servant (IPC 499 Ex.2 / BNS 356)
Third Exception: conduct of any person touching a public question (IPC 499 Ex.3 / BNS 356)
Fourth Exception: substantially true report of court proceedings (IPC 499 Ex.4 / BNS 356)
Fifth to Tenth Exceptions: merits of cases, merits of public performance, censure by authority, complaint to authority, good faith caution, imputation in good faith (IPC 499 Exs.5-10 / BNS 356)
Consent of the person defamed
Community service as alternative punishment under BNS 356
CONCEPT_DEFAMATION.key_phrases: "defamation", "Section 499", "Section 500", "BNS 356", "imputation concerning", "harm the reputation", "by words either spoken or intended to be read", "lowering moral or intellectual character", "community service"
CONCEPT_DEFAMATION.establishing_cases: Subramanian Swamy v. Union of India (2016), R. Rajagopal v. State of TN (1994), Kiran Bedi v. Committee of Inquiry (1989)

CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_INTIMIDATION.label: Criminal Intimidation
requires: [AND]
The accused threatens another person
The threat is to cause: injury to the person, reputation, or property of the threatened person OR to the person, reputation, or property of anyone in whom the threatened person is interested

The threat is made with intent to cause alarm to the person threatened, OR to cause that person to do any act which they are not legally bound to do, or omit to do any act which they are legally entitled to do
defeaters: Threat was not to cause injury — mere expression of displeasure or warning
No intent to cause alarm or to coerce the threatened person
The threatened act was something the person was legally bound to do anyway
Conditional threat that is lawful (e.g., legitimate legal action)
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), minority (IPC 82-83 / BNS 17-18)
CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_INTIMIDATION.typical_edge_pattern: (Accused) --[threatens injury to]--> (Person/Reputation/Property) --[with intent to cause alarm or coerce]--> (Conviction_IPC503-506/BNS351)
CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_INTIMIDATION.key_phrases: "criminal intimidation", "Section 503", "Section 506", "BNS 351", "threatens to cause injury", "intent to cause alarm", "threat of death", "threat of grievous hurt", "extortion by threat"
CONCEPT_CRIMINAL_INTIMIDATION.establishing_cases: Manik Taneja v. State of Karnataka (2015), Narayan Dutt Tiwari v. State of UP (2014), State of WB v. Mohit Mondal (2019)

CONCEPT_ATTEMPT.label: Attempt
requires: [AND]
The accused intended to commit a particular offense
The accused did an act towards the commission of that offense
The act went beyond mere preparation — it was a direct move towards commission
The offense was not completed (if completed, the substantive offense is charged)
The offense attempted must be one punishable with imprisonment (IPC 511 / BNS 62)
defeaters: Act was merely preparatory and did not cross into the attempt stage
Voluntary withdrawal/abandonment before the act becomes an attempt (contested in Indian law — not a statutory defense but may be relevant)
Impossibility — factual impossibility is generally not a defense; legal impossibility may be (State of Maharashtra v. Mohd. Yakub, 1980)
General defenses: insanity (IPC 84 / BNS 22), minority (IPC 82-83 / BNS 17-18)
Right of private defence (IPC 96-106 / BNS 34-44)
CONCEPT_ATTEMPT.key_phrases: "attempt to commit", "Section 511", "BNS 62", "last act done towards commission", "beyond mere preparation", "not completed", "attempted to commit", "half of the longest term of imprisonment"
CONCEPT_ATTEMPT.establishing_cases: State of Maharashtra v. Mohd. Yakub (1980), Malkiat Singh v. State of Punjab (1970), Abhayanand Mishra v. State of Bihar (1961), Queen Empress v. Lizzie Donohew (1886)

CONCEPT_ARREST_WITHOUT_WARRANT.label: Arrest Without Warrant
notes: **35(7): No arrest for infirm/elderly (60+) for offences punishable <3 years without prior permission of an officer not below the rank of Deputy Superintendent of Police**
requires: [OR — any of the following grounds under CrPC 41 / BNSS 35]
Person commits a cognizable offense in the presence of a police officer

Credible information or reasonable suspicion that the person has committed a cognizable offense punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years — AND the officer has reason to believe arrest is necessary to: prevent further offense, ensure proper investigation, prevent tampering with evidence, prevent influencing witnesses, ensure court appearance (CrPC 41(1)(b)(ii) / BNSS 35)
Person is a proclaimed offender
Person possesses stolen property or is reasonably suspected of having it
Person obstructs a police officer in duty or escapes lawful custody
Person is a military deserter
Person subject to an order under another law requiring arrest
BNSS 35(7): No arrest for infirm/elderly (60+) for offences punishable with less than 3 years without prior permission of officer not below DSP rank
defeaters: Offense is non-cognizable (arrest requires warrant unless exceptions apply)
The conditions under CrPC 41(1)(b)(ii) / BNSS 35 are not satisfied — arrest is not necessary
Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014) guidelines not followed for offenses with punishment up to 7 years: checklist/notice of appearance not served
BNSS 35(7) protection: person is elderly (60+)/infirm and offense punishable with less than 3 years, and DSP permission not obtained
Arrest is arbitrary or in violation of Article 22 (grounds of arrest not communicated)
CONCEPT_ARREST_WITHOUT_WARRANT.typical_edge_pattern: (Cognizable_Offense) --[credible information / in presence of officer]--> (Grounds for Arrest Exist + Necessity Established) --[arrest made]--> (Arrest_CrPC41/BNSS35)
CONCEPT_ARREST_WITHOUT_WARRANT.key_phrases: "arrest without warrant", "Section 41", "BNSS 35", "cognizable offense", "credible information", "reason to believe", "Arnesh Kumar guidelines", "necessity of arrest", "notice of appearance", "infirm or elderly"
CONCEPT_ARREST_WITHOUT_WARRANT.establishing_cases: Joginder Kumar v. State of UP (1994), D.K. Basu v. State of WB (1997), Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014), Lalita Kumari v. Government of UP (2014), Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI (2022)

CONCEPT_MAINTENANCE.label: Maintenance
requires: [AND]

Applicant belongs to a specified category: wife (including divorced wife who has not remarried), legitimate or illegitimate minor child (whether married or not), legitimate or illegitimate major child unable to maintain themselves by reason of physical or mental abnormality, father or mother unable to maintain themselves
The person against whom maintenance is claimed has sufficient means
The person against whom maintenance is claimed has refused or neglected to maintain the applicant
Application is made before a Magistrate of the first class (CrPC 125 / BNSS 144)
defeaters: Wife is living in adultery (CrPC 125(4) / BNSS 144)
Wife has refused to live with her husband without sufficient reason (CrPC 125(4) / BNSS 144)
Wife and husband are living separately by mutual consent (CrPC 125(4) / BNSS 144)
Divorced wife has remarried
Divorced wife has received the whole of the sum agreed upon or ordered as customary payment on divorce
Child has attained majority and is able to maintain themselves (not applicable to handicapped children)
The person from whom maintenance is claimed does not have sufficient means
CONCEPT_MAINTENANCE.typical_edge_pattern: (Applicant_Wife/Child/Parent) --[unable to maintain self]--> (Person with Sufficient Means Neglects/Refuses) --[application to Magistrate]--> (Maintenance_Order_CrPC125/BNSS144)
CONCEPT_MAINTENANCE.key_phrases: "maintenance", "Section 125", "BNSS 144", "neglect or refusal to maintain", "wife unable to maintain herself", "sufficient means", "interim maintenance", "maintenance of wife, children and parents"
CONCEPT_MAINTENANCE.establishing_cases: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum (1985), Rajnesh v. Neha (2021), Chaturbhuj v. Sita Bai (2008), Kamala v. M.R. Mohanan (2021)

CONCEPT_FIR.label: Fir
notes: **Electronic filing explicit; Zero FIR statutory recognition; 173(3): preliminary inquiry for 3-7yr offences with DSP approval**
requires: [AND]
Information relating to the commission of a cognizable offense is received
The information is given to an officer in charge of a police station (CrPC 154 / BNSS 173)
If given orally, it must be reduced to writing and read over to the informant and signed by the informant
A copy of the FIR must be given to the informant free of cost
The substance of the information must be entered in a book to be kept in the prescribed form (station diary / daily diary)

Under BNSS 173: electronic filing of FIR is explicitly recognized; Zero FIR (FIR can be filed at any police station regardless of jurisdiction) has statutory recognition; BNSS 173(3): preliminary inquiry permitted for offenses punishable between 3-7 years with DSP approval
defeaters: Information does not disclose a cognizable offense (police may refuse to register FIR — but Lalita Kumari mandates registration if cognizable offense is disclosed)
Information is vague or does not contain sufficient detail to constitute a cognizable offense
Court-ordered FIR registration under CrPC 156(3) / BNSS 175(3) — if police refuse, Magistrate can direct registration
FIR is an abuse of process — quashable under CrPC 482 / BNSS 528
CONCEPT_FIR.key_phrases: "First Information Report", "FIR", "Section 154", "BNSS 173", "cognizable offense", "information in writing", "Zero FIR", "electronic filing", "preliminary inquiry", "station diary entry", "duty to register"
CONCEPT_FIR.establishing_cases: Lalita Kumari v. Government of UP (2014), State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal (1992), T.T. Antony v. State of Kerala (2001), CBI v. Tapan Kumar Singh (2003)

CONCEPT_POLICE_EXAMINATION.label: Police Examination
requires: [AND]
Investigation of a cognizable offense is in progress
The police officer investigating the case may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case (CrPC 161 / BNSS 180)
Such person is bound to answer truly all questions (except answers that would expose them to a criminal charge, penalty, or forfeiture)
The police officer shall reduce the statement to writing (if deemed necessary)
The statement shall not be signed by the person making it (CrPC 162 / BNSS 181)
defeaters: Person is not bound to answer questions that have a tendency to expose them to a criminal charge (right against self-incrimination — Article 20(3))
Statement not recorded in the manner prescribed — irregularity may affect evidentiary value
Statement to police is not admissible as substantive evidence (CrPC 162 / BNSS 181) — can only be used for contradiction during cross-examination
CONCEPT_POLICE_EXAMINATION.key_phrases: "police examination", "Section 161", "BNSS 180", "Section 162", "BNSS 181", "statement to police", "bound to answer truly", "not signed by person", "not admissible as substantive evidence", "used for contradiction"
CONCEPT_POLICE_EXAMINATION.establishing_cases: Tahsildar Singh v. State of UP (1959), State of UP v. Singhara Singh (1964), Rattan Singh v. State of HP (1997)

CONCEPT_CONFESSION_RECORDING.label: Confession Recording
notes: **BNSS 183: confessions/statements may also be recorded by audio-video electronic means (in the presence of the accused’s advocate).**
requires: [AND]
Confession or statement must be recorded by a Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate (CrPC 164 / BNSS 183)
Before recording, the Magistrate must explain to the person that they are not bound to make a confession and that if they do, it may be used as evidence against them
The Magistrate must satisfy themselves that the confession is being made voluntarily and not under any inducement, threat, or promise
The confession must be recorded in the manner provided for recording evidence (CrPC 281 / BNSS 316)
A memorandum must be made at the foot of the record signed by the Magistrate
Under BNSS 183: confession/statement may also be recorded by audio-video electronic means in the presence of the accused's advocate
defeaters: Confession not recorded by a Magistrate — inadmissible (CrPC 164 / BNSS 183)
Magistrate failed to explain that the confession was voluntary and need not be made
Insufficient cooling-off period — reflection time not given (minimum 24 hours typically required by courts)
Evidence of inducement, threat, promise, or coercion affecting voluntariness
Procedural non-compliance (unsigned, not read over, advocate not present for audio-video recording under BNSS)
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_RECORDING.key_phrases: "confession recorded under Section 164", "BNSS 183", "judicial confession", "voluntary confession", "before a Magistrate", "not under inducement threat or promise", "audio-video recording", "cooling off period"
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_RECORDING.establishing_cases: Sarwan Singh v. State of Punjab (1957), Shivappa v. State of Karnataka (1995), Ram Singh v. Sonia (2007), State of UP v. Singhara Singh (1964)

CONCEPT_ACCUSED_EXAMINATION.label: Accused Examination
requires: [AND]
The prosecution evidence has been completed / prosecution closes its case
The court must examine the accused personally (CrPC 313 / BNSS 351)
The purpose is to enable the accused to explain any circumstances appearing in evidence against them
The accused shall not be administered an oath while being examined
The accused may refuse to answer or give false answers — no penalty for false answers (but court may draw adverse inference)
The examination must cover each material circumstance/incriminating material appearing against the accused
defeaters: Failure to put material circumstances to the accused vitiates the trial — prejudice must be shown (may lead to retrial)
Omission to examine under Section 313/351 is an irregularity, not illegality per se — depends on whether prejudice was caused
Accused's right to silence under Article 20(3) — but Section 313/351 is not hit by Article 20(3) as it is not on oath
CONCEPT_ACCUSED_EXAMINATION.key_phrases: "examination of accused", "Section 313", "BNSS 351", "personally examined", "explain circumstances", "not on oath", "each material circumstance", "opportunity to explain incriminating evidence", "adverse inference"
CONCEPT_ACCUSED_EXAMINATION.establishing_cases: Shivaji Sahabrao Bobade v. State of Maharashtra (1973), Reena Hazarika v. State of Assam (2019), Basavaraj R. Patil v. State of Karnataka (2000), Asraf Ali v. State of Assam (2008)

CONCEPT_ADD_ACCUSED.label: Add Accused
requires: [AND]
A trial is in progress before a court (Sessions Court or Magistrate)

From the evidence adduced during trial, it appears that a person not being the accused has committed the offense for which the accused is being tried or an offense connected with or arising out of the same facts
The court may proceed against such person for the offense (CrPC 319 / BNSS 358)
The power can be exercised at any stage of the inquiry or trial before judgment is pronounced
The standard of satisfaction is higher than prima facie — court must be satisfied that there is sufficient material/evidence to proceed
defeaters: Evidence does not disclose prima facie case against the proposed accused
Trial has concluded and judgment has been pronounced — power cannot be exercised post-judgment
Power exercised mechanically without application of mind
Proposed accused has already been investigated and deliberately discharged / not charge-sheeted — court must still evaluate evidence independently
CONCEPT_ADD_ACCUSED.typical_edge_pattern: (Trial_in_Progress) --[evidence reveals involvement of other person]--> (Court_Exercises_CrPC319/BNSS358) --[summoning order]--> (New_Accused_Added_to_Trial)
CONCEPT_ADD_ACCUSED.key_phrases: "power to add accused", "Section 319", "BNSS 358", "proceed against other persons", "evidence during trial", "summoning additional accused", "any person not being the accused", "same transaction"
CONCEPT_ADD_ACCUSED.establishing_cases: Hardeep Singh v. State of Punjab (2014), S. Mohammed Ispahani v. Yogendra Shukla (1946), Sarabjit Singh v. State of Punjab (2009), Rajesh v. State of Haryana (2019)

CONCEPT_APPEAL_ACQUITTAL.label: Appeal Acquittal
requires: [AND]
An order of acquittal has been passed by a court

Appeal is filed by: the State Government against acquittal in cases instituted on complaint by the State or on police report (CrPC 378(1) / BNSS 419(1)); OR the complainant against acquittal (CrPC 378(4) / BNSS 419(4) — with leave of the court); OR the Central Government in certain cases

The appeal must demonstrate that the acquittal is: perverse, or based on erroneous appreciation of evidence, or involves misreading/non-consideration of material evidence, or the trial court's view was not a possible view on the evidence
Appellate court's power to interfere with acquittal is narrower than with conviction — requires substantial and compelling reasons
defeaters: Acquittal is based on a reasonable/plausible view of evidence — appellate court should not disturb if two views are possible
Presumption of innocence reinforced by acquittal — double presumption
Appeal filed beyond limitation period
No leave granted to complainant under CrPC 378(4) / BNSS 419(4)
No substantial or compelling reasons to overturn acquittal
CONCEPT_APPEAL_ACQUITTAL.key_phrases: "appeal against acquittal", "Section 378", "BNSS 419", "perverse acquittal", "two views possible", "substantial and compelling reasons", "misappreciation of evidence", "double presumption of innocence", "leave to appeal"
CONCEPT_APPEAL_ACQUITTAL.establishing_cases: Chandrappa v. State of Karnataka (2007), Atley v. State of UP (1955), Sanwat Singh v. State of Rajasthan (1961), K. Gopal Reddy v. State of AP (1979)

CONCEPT_REGULAR_BAIL.label: Regular Bail
requires: [AND]
The accused is in custody (arrested or in judicial custody)

Application for bail is made before: Magistrate's Court under CrPC 437 / BNSS 480 (for non-bailable offenses not punishable with death or life imprisonment); OR Court of Session / High Court under CrPC 439 / BNSS 483

Court considers: nature and gravity of the offense, character/antecedents of the accused, likelihood of fleeing from justice, likelihood of tampering with evidence or influencing witnesses, reasonable grounds for believing the accused is not guilty
For offenses not punishable with death or life imprisonment: bail is the rule, jail is the exception
defeaters: Reasonable grounds exist to believe the accused is guilty of an offense punishable with death or life imprisonment (CrPC 437(1) proviso / BNSS 480(1) proviso)
Previous conviction for offense punishable with death, life, or 7+ years
Likelihood of absconding or fleeing justice
Likelihood of tampering with evidence or influencing witnesses
Risk to the complainant or victim
Accused may commit similar offense if released
Investigation may be prejudiced
CONCEPT_REGULAR_BAIL.key_phrases: "regular bail", "Section 437", "BNSS 480", "bail is the rule, jail is the exception", "reasonable grounds", "nature and gravity of accusation", "likelihood of absconding", "tampering with evidence", "non-bailable offense"
CONCEPT_REGULAR_BAIL.establishing_cases: Sanjay Chandra v. CBI (2012), State of Rajasthan v. Balchand (1977), Gudikanti Narasimhulu v. Public Prosecutor (1978), Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI (2022)

CONCEPT_UNDERTRIAL_DETENTION.label: Undertrial Detention
notes: **First-time offender: bail on bond after 1/3 of maximum sentence; 479(2): multiple pending cases = ineligible**
requires: [AND — conditions for mandatory bail under CrPC 436A / BNSS 479]
The accused is an undertrial prisoner
The accused has undergone detention for a period extending to one-half of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for the offense charged (CrPC 436A / BNSS 479)
The court shall release the person on personal bond with or without sureties
Under BNSS 479: first-time offender is entitled to bail on personal bond after serving one-third of the maximum sentence
BNSS 479(2): ineligible if the person has other pending cases — multiple pending cases disqualify the person
defeaters: The offense charged is punishable with death — Section 436A/479 does not apply
Court records reasons in writing why the person should not be released
Multiple pending cases against the undertrial (BNSS 479(2))
Public Prosecutor opposes with material showing release would prejudice ongoing trial
CONCEPT_UNDERTRIAL_DETENTION.typical_edge_pattern: (Undertrial_Detained) --[served half of max sentence]--> (Mandatory_Bail_CrPC436A/BNSS479) --[unless death penalty offense or multiple cases]--> (Release_on_Bond)
CONCEPT_UNDERTRIAL_DETENTION.key_phrases: "undertrial detention", "Section 436A", "BNSS 479", "half of the maximum period", "one-third for first-time offender", "personal bond", "default bail", "statutory bail", "mandatory release", "multiple pending cases"
CONCEPT_UNDERTRIAL_DETENTION.establishing_cases: Hussain v. Union of India (2017), Bhim Singh v. Union of India (2015), Re: Inhuman Conditions in 1382 Prisons (2016), Supreme Court Legal Aid Committee v. Union of India (1994)

CONCEPT_ANTICIPATORY_BAIL.label: Anticipatory Bail
notes: **482(4): Anticipatory bail does not apply to arrests for offences under BNS s.65 and BNS s.70(2). (Verify other sub-section differences separately.)**
requires: [AND]
The applicant has reason to believe that they may be arrested on an accusation of having committed a non-bailable offense
Application is made before: the High Court or the Court of Session (CrPC 438 / BNSS 482)
The court considers: nature and gravity of the accusation, antecedents of the applicant, possibility of applicant fleeing justice, whether the accusation is made to humiliate/injure the applicant
Court may grant bail with conditions: availability for interrogation, not making inducements/threats to witnesses, not leaving India without permission, other conditions under CrPC 438(2) / BNSS 482
BNSS 482(4): anticipatory bail does NOT apply to offenses under BNS 65 (rape of minor under 16) and BNS 70(2) (gang rape of minor)
defeaters: Accusation is for an offense excluded from anticipatory bail (BNS 65 / BNS 70(2) under BNSS 482(4))
Prima facie case of serious nature against applicant — custodial interrogation necessary
Applicant is likely to flee jurisdiction
Applicant is likely to tamper with evidence or influence witnesses
Apprehension of arrest is not genuine or bona fide
Accusation involves offenses against women/children where custodial investigation is essential
CONCEPT_ANTICIPATORY_BAIL.key_phrases: "anticipatory bail", "Section 438", "BNSS 482", "reason to believe arrest may be made", "pre-arrest bail", "non-bailable offense", "direction for release on bail", "custodial interrogation", "humiliate or injure"
CONCEPT_ANTICIPATORY_BAIL.establishing_cases: Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab (1980), Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi) (2020), Siddharam Satlingappa Mhetre v. State of Maharashtra (2011), Bhadresh Bipinbhai Sheth v. State of Gujarat (2016)

CONCEPT_BAIL_HC_SESSIONS.label: Bail Hc Sessions
requires: [AND]
The accused is in custody or has been released on bail by a lower court
Application for bail is made before the High Court or Court of Session (CrPC 439 / BNSS 483)
The HC/Sessions Court has wide discretionary power — broader than that of Magistrate under CrPC 437 / BNSS 480

Court considers: prima facie case, gravity of the charge, severity of punishment, likelihood of absconding, character/standing of accused, health and age of accused, whether the accused was involved in other crimes
HC/Sessions Court can grant bail even in cases punishable with death or life imprisonment (unlike Magistrate)
defeaters: Very serious nature of offense and strong prima facie case
Likelihood of absconding or fleeing justice
Likelihood of tampering with evidence or influencing witnesses
Risk of repetition of similar offenses
Bail conditions violated (bail can be cancelled under CrPC 439(2) / BNSS 483(2))
Trial court has already granted/refused bail on proper consideration — appellate court should not interfere unless perverse
CONCEPT_BAIL_HC_SESSIONS.key_phrases: "Section 439", "BNSS 483", "bail by High Court", "bail by Sessions Court", "special powers", "cancel bail", "wider discretion", "prima facie case", "gravity of charge", "bail pending trial"
CONCEPT_BAIL_HC_SESSIONS.establishing_cases: State of Rajasthan v. Balchand (1977), Kalyan Chandra Sarkar v. Rajesh Ranjan (2004), Puran v. Rambilas (2001), Neeru Yadav v. State of UP (2014)

CONCEPT_INHERENT_POWERS_HC.label: Inherent Powers Hc
requires: [OR — three purposes of inherent power under CrPC 482 / BNSS 528]
To give effect to any order under the Code (CrPC / BNSS)
To prevent abuse of the process of any court
To secure the ends of justice
The power is exercisable only by the High Court
The power is inherent — it is not conferred by the Code but saved/preserved by CrPC 482 / BNSS 528
defeaters: Express provision in the Code covers the situation — inherent power should not be exercised if a specific remedy exists
Exercise of inherent power would be contrary to express provisions of the Code
Matter involves disputed questions of fact requiring trial (quashing inappropriate)
FIR/complaint discloses a cognizable offense on its face — the bar in State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal (1992) categories not met
Frivolous or vexatious application for quashing
CONCEPT_INHERENT_POWERS_HC.typical_edge_pattern: (Abuse_of_Process / Injustice) --[petition under CrPC482/BNSS528]--> (HC_Inherent_Power) --[if ends of justice require]--> (FIR/Complaint/Proceedings_Quashed)
CONCEPT_INHERENT_POWERS_HC.key_phrases: "inherent powers", "Section 482", "BNSS 528", "quashing of FIR", "quashing of complaint", "abuse of process", "ends of justice", "nothing in this Code shall limit or affect the inherent powers", "prevent abuse"
CONCEPT_INHERENT_POWERS_HC.establishing_cases: State of Haryana v. Bhajan Lal (1992), R.P. Kapur v. State of Punjab (1960), Parbatbhai Aahir v. State of Gujarat (2017), Neeharika Infrastructure v. State of Maharashtra (2021)

CONCEPT_DEFINITIONS.label: Definitions
requires: [AND — foundational definitions under IEA 3 / BSA 2]
Key definitions that underpin the entire evidence framework:
"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons legally authorized to take evidence (IEA 3 / BSA 2)
"Fact" means anything capable of being perceived by the senses or any mental condition of which a person is conscious (IEA 3 / BSA 2)
"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures, or marks — BSA 2 explicitly includes electronic and digital records

"Evidence" means and includes: (i) all statements which the court permits or requires to be made before it (oral evidence), and (ii) all documents including electronic records produced for inspection (documentary evidence) (IEA 3 / BSA 2)
"Relevant" fact is one connected with another in the ways described in sections relating to relevancy
"Proved" / "Disproved" / "Not proved" — three degrees of proof defined
defeaters: Definitions are foundational and do not have 'defeaters' per se — they are definitional provisions
Challenge may arise if a particular item does not fall within the statutory definition (e.g., whether a particular electronic record qualifies as a 'document')
Ambiguity in application of definitions to novel contexts (digital evidence, AI-generated records)
CONCEPT_DEFINITIONS.typical_edge_pattern: (Fact/Document/Evidence) --[meets definition under IEA 3/BSA 2]--> (Classifiable as Evidence) --[if relevant]--> (Admissible_Subject_to_Rules)
CONCEPT_DEFINITIONS.key_phrases: "definitions", "Section 3 IEA", "BSA 2", "document", "electronic record", "fact", "evidence", "proved", "disproved", "not proved", "relevant fact", "oral evidence", "documentary evidence"
CONCEPT_DEFINITIONS.establishing_cases: Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014) — electronic record as document, State of Maharashtra v. Praful B. Desai (2003) — video evidence as testimony, Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal (2020) — definition of electronic evidence

CONCEPT_RES_GESTAE.label: Res Gestae
requires: [AND — under IEA 6 / BSA 4]
Facts that form part of the same transaction as the fact in issue
The facts must be so connected with the fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction (whether they occurred at the same time and place, or at different times and places)
Under BSA 4: 'or relevant facts' has been added — broadening the scope slightly
Spontaneity — the statement or act must be contemporaneous with the main event and be a spontaneous reaction, not a result of afterthought or reflection
defeaters: Statement or fact is not part of the same transaction — there is a temporal or logical gap
Statement was made after time for reflection/fabrication — not spontaneous
Statement is a narrative of a past event, not a reaction to it
The connection between the fact and the fact in issue is too remote
CONCEPT_RES_GESTAE.typical_edge_pattern: (Statement/Fact) --[contemporaneous with and forms part of same transaction]--> (Fact_in_Issue) --[admissible as res gestae]--> (Evidence_IEA6/BSA4)
CONCEPT_RES_GESTAE.key_phrases: "res gestae", "Section 6", "BSA 4", "part of the same transaction", "contemporaneous", "spontaneous", "forming part of same transaction", "too closely connected to be separated"
CONCEPT_RES_GESTAE.establishing_cases: R v. Bedingfield (1879) (distinguished in Indian law), Gentela Vijayavardhan Rao v. State of AP (1996), Sukhar v. State of UP (1999), Patel Hiralal Joitaram v. State of Gujarat (2001)

CONCEPT_CONFESSION_ADMISSIBILITY.label: Confession Admissibility
requires: [AND — under IEA 24-29 / BSA 22]
The confession must be relevant (an admission by accused of the offense charged)
The confession must be voluntary — not caused by any inducement, threat, or promise (IEA 24 / BSA 22)
The inducement, threat, or promise must have reference to the charge against the accused
The inducement, threat, or promise must be from a person in authority
Under BSA 22: 'coercion' has been explicitly added alongside inducement, threat, and promise
If initially involuntary, it becomes admissible if the court believes the impression caused by the inducement/threat/promise has been fully removed (IEA 28 / BSA 22)
defeaters: Confession was caused by inducement, threat, promise, or coercion from a person in authority (IEA 24 / BSA 22) — inadmissible
Confession was made to a police officer — inadmissible (IEA 25 / BSA 23)
Confession was made while in police custody — inadmissible unless made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate (IEA 26 / BSA 23)
Retracted confession — admissible but requires corroboration as a matter of prudence
Co-accused confession — not substantive evidence against other accused, only corroborative (IEA 30 / BSA 24)
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_ADMISSIBILITY.typical_edge_pattern: (Confession_by_Accused) --[voluntary, not under inducement/threat/promise/coercion]--> (Not to Police / In Presence of Magistrate) --[admissible]--> (Evidence_IEA24-29/BSA22)
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_ADMISSIBILITY.key_phrases: "confession", "admissibility of confession", "Section 24", "Section 25", "Section 26", "BSA 22", "inducement threat or promise", "coercion", "voluntary confession", "person in authority", "retracted confession"
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_ADMISSIBILITY.establishing_cases: Pakala Narayana Swami v. Emperor (1939), State of UP v. Deoman Upadhyaya (1960), Nandini Satpathy v. P.L. Dani (1978), Kartar Singh v. State of Punjab (1994)

CONCEPT_CONFESSION_POLICE.label: Confession Police
requires: [AND — the exclusionary rule under IEA 25-26 / BSA 23]
The statement amounts to a confession (admission of guilt for the offense charged)
The confession is made to a police officer (IEA 25 / BSA 23) — absolutely inadmissible regardless of voluntariness
OR the confession is made while the person is in police custody (IEA 26 / BSA 23) — inadmissible UNLESS made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate
The rationale is protection against police coercion and torture
defeaters: Statement does not amount to a confession — it is merely an admission or exculpatory statement (admissible as such)
Confession made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate even while in police custody (IEA 26 exception / BSA 23)
Discovery of fact pursuant to confession — that portion is admissible under IEA 27 / BSA 23 proviso (the discovery, not the confession itself)
Person making statement is not a 'police officer' as defined in law (revenue officers, customs officers may or may not qualify — R.S. Nayak v. A.R. Antulay, 1984)
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_POLICE.key_phrases: "confession to police", "Section 25", "Section 26", "BSA 23", "inadmissible", "police custody", "immediate presence of Magistrate", "police officer", "exclusionary rule", "protection against police coercion"
CONCEPT_CONFESSION_POLICE.establishing_cases: Dagdu v. State of Maharashtra (1977), State of UP v. Deoman Upadhyaya (1960), R.S. Nayak v. A.R. Antulay (1984), Aghnoo Nagesia v. State of Bihar (1966)

CONCEPT_DISCOVERY_CONFESSION.label: Discovery Confession
requires: [AND — under IEA 27 / BSA 23 proviso]
An accused person is in police custody
The accused makes a statement (which may otherwise be inadmissible as a confession to police)
The statement leads to the discovery of a fact
Only so much of the statement as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered is admissible
The discovery must be of a fact not previously known to the police
The fact must actually be discovered as a consequence of the information
defeaters: The fact was already known to the police or had been discovered independently — no 'discovery' pursuant to the statement
The entire confession is sought to be admitted — only the portion distinctly relating to the discovery is admissible
The connection between the statement and the discovery is not established
The discovery is planted or staged by the police
The object/fact supposedly discovered was in an accessible place and could have been found without the statement
CONCEPT_DISCOVERY_CONFESSION.typical_edge_pattern: (Statement_by_Accused_in_Custody) --[leads to]--> (Discovery_of_Fact) --[only portion relating to fact discovered admissible]--> (Admissible_IEA27/BSA23_Proviso)
CONCEPT_DISCOVERY_CONFESSION.key_phrases: "discovery pursuant to confession", "Section 27", "BSA 23 proviso", "fact discovered", "so much of the information", "distinctly relates to the fact thereby discovered", "pointing out", "recovery of weapon", "discovery memo"
CONCEPT_DISCOVERY_CONFESSION.establishing_cases: Pulukuri Kottaya v. Emperor (1947), State of UP v. Deoman Upadhyaya (1960), Anter Singh v. State of Rajasthan (2004), Geejaganda Nagesha v. State of Karnataka (2019)

CONCEPT_CO_ACCUSED_CONFESSION.label: Co Accused Confession
requires: [AND — under IEA 30 / BSA 24]
Two or more persons are being tried jointly for the same offense
One of the co-accused makes a confession affecting himself AND another co-accused
The confession is proved (i.e., properly recorded and admissible against the maker)
The court MAY take into consideration such confession against the other co-accused as well as the maker
Under BSA 24: trial in absentia is treated as a joint trial for the purposes of this section
defeaters: Co-accused's confession is not substantive evidence — it is only a 'relevant consideration', not evidence per se (Kashmira Singh v. State of MP, 1952)
Confession of co-accused alone cannot form the basis of conviction — requires independent corroboration
Co-accused are not being tried jointly — Section 30/24 does not apply
Confession is retracted and there is no corroboration
Confession is unreliable or obtained improperly (inadmissible even against the maker)
CONCEPT_CO_ACCUSED_CONFESSION.key_phrases: "co-accused confession", "Section 30", "BSA 24", "jointly tried", "confession affecting co-accused", "not substantive evidence", "requires corroboration", "relevant consideration", "trial in absentia"
CONCEPT_CO_ACCUSED_CONFESSION.establishing_cases: Kashmira Singh v. State of MP (1952), Hari Charan Kurmi v. State of Bihar (1964), Bhuboni Sahu v. The King (1949), Nathu v. State of UP (1956)

CONCEPT_DYING_DECLARATION.label: Dying Declaration
requires: [AND — under IEA 32(1) / BSA 26]
The declarant has died (or the statement is by a person whose death is the subject of inquiry)
The statement relates to the cause of the declarant's death OR relates to any of the circumstances of the transaction which resulted in the death
The statement was made by the person who was under the expectation of death (though actual expectation of death is not strictly required under Indian law unlike English law)
Under BSA 26: restructured into 8 subsections for clarity
The statement can be oral, written, or by gestures (if the person is unable to speak)
defeaters: Declarant did not actually die and the case is not one where their death is in question (IEA 32 / BSA 26 does not apply)
Statement does not relate to the cause of death or circumstances of the transaction resulting in death
Statement is unreliable — declarant was in a state of mind where they could not make a coherent/truthful statement (unconscious, heavily medicated, tutored)
Multiple dying declarations that are contradictory — the most reliable one is accepted
Statement is not recorded properly (no attestation, no doctor's fitness certificate though not mandatory, suspicious circumstances)
Dying declaration is a result of tutoring or prompting by interested parties
CONCEPT_DYING_DECLARATION.typical_edge_pattern: (Person_Dies) --[had made statement about cause/circumstances of death]--> (Dying_Declaration_IEA32/BSA26) --[if reliable and consistent]--> (Substantive_Evidence_Sufficient_for_Conviction)
CONCEPT_DYING_DECLARATION.key_phrases: "dying declaration", "Section 32", "BSA 26", "cause of death", "circumstances of the transaction resulting in death", "statement of deceased", "expectation of death", "fit state of mind", "sole basis for conviction"
CONCEPT_DYING_DECLARATION.establishing_cases: Laxman v. State of Maharashtra (2002), Pakala Narayana Swami v. Emperor (1939), Khushal Rao v. State of Bombay (1958), Paniben v. State of Gujarat (1992)

CONCEPT_EXPERT_OPINION.label: Expert Opinion
notes: **"Any other field" added; Examiner of Electronic Evidence (IT Act) recognized**
requires: [AND — under IEA 45 / BSA 39]
The court needs an opinion on a point of: foreign law, science, art, handwriting, finger impressions, or 'any other field' (added by BSA 39)
The opinion is given by a person specially skilled in such field (expert)
The expert's opinion is relevant and admissible as evidence
Under BSA 39: Examiner of Electronic Evidence under the IT Act is recognized as an expert
Expert must be qualified by training, study, or experience in the relevant field
defeaters: Expert is not qualified in the relevant field — lacks necessary training or experience
Expert opinion is not corroborated by other evidence — expert opinion is advisory, not binding on the court
Expert opinion is based on incomplete or incorrect data/material
Opposing expert opinion creates genuine doubt — court must weigh competing opinions
Expert opinion on a point that is not beyond the competence of the court — court is the ultimate expert on law
Expert has a bias or interest in the outcome
CONCEPT_EXPERT_OPINION.key_phrases: "expert opinion", "Section 45", "BSA 39", "specially skilled", "opinion of expert", "handwriting expert", "forensic expert", "medical opinion", "Examiner of Electronic Evidence", "corroboration required"
CONCEPT_EXPERT_OPINION.establishing_cases: Ramesh Kumar Dey v. State of Tripura (2014), State of HP v. Jai Lal (1999), Murari Lal v. State of MP (1980), Dodhi Amma v. K. Ayyasami (2001)

CONCEPT_ELECTRONIC_EVIDENCE.label: Electronic Evidence
notes: **MAJOR: "Communication device" explicit; MANDATORY certificate in prescribed format; HASH value required; signed by person in charge AND expert**
requires: [AND — under IEA 65B / BSA 63]
The evidence is an electronic record (as defined in IT Act 2000)
The electronic record must be accompanied by a certificate under IEA 65B(4) / BSA 63(4)

The certificate must: identify the electronic record, describe the manner of its production, give particulars of the device used, and be signed by a person occupying a responsible official position in relation to the device
Under BSA 63: certificate must be in the prescribed format; hash value of the electronic record is MANDATORY; certificate must be signed BOTH by the person in charge of the device AND by an expert
The computer output was produced during the period when the computer was regularly used for lawful activities, and the information was regularly fed into the computer in the ordinary course
defeaters: No certificate under IEA 65B(4) / BSA 63(4) — electronic evidence is inadmissible (Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer, 2014)
Certificate does not comply with prescribed format or hash value requirement (under BSA 63)
Electronic record has been tampered with — hash value mismatch
Chain of custody of the electronic device/record not established
Computer was not functioning properly during the relevant period
The original electronic record is available and secondary evidence rules should not apply (primary evidence preferred)
Certificate signed by an unqualified or unauthorized person
CONCEPT_ELECTRONIC_EVIDENCE.typical_edge_pattern: (Electronic_Record) --[with valid 65B/63 certificate + hash value]--> (Admissible_Electronic_Evidence) --[court evaluates authenticity]--> (Evidence_Weighed)
CONCEPT_ELECTRONIC_EVIDENCE.key_phrases: "electronic evidence", "Section 65B", "BSA 63", "certificate", "hash value", "electronic record", "computer output", "communication device", "digital evidence", "Anvar P.V. compliance", "prescribed format"
CONCEPT_ELECTRONIC_EVIDENCE.establishing_cases: Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014), Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal (2020), Shafhi Mohammad v. State of HP (2018), Tomaso Bruno v. State of UP (2015) (declared per incuriam by Khotkar, 2020)

CONCEPT_BURDEN_OF_PROOF.label: Burden Of Proof
requires: [AND — under IEA 101-104 / BSA 104-107]
The burden of proof lies on the person who asserts a fact (IEA 101 / BSA 104)
In criminal cases: the prosecution bears the burden of proving guilt beyond reasonable doubt
The burden of proof as to a particular fact lies on the person who wishes the court to believe in its existence (IEA 103 / BSA 106)
The burden of proving any fact especially within the knowledge of a person lies on that person (IEA 106 / BSA 109)
The standard of proof in criminal cases is 'beyond reasonable doubt'; in civil cases it is 'preponderance of probabilities'
defeaters: Statutory presumptions shift the burden to the accused (e.g., IPC 304B / BNS 80 for dowry death presumption under IEA 113B / BSA 118)
Reverse onus clauses in special legislation (NDPS Act, Prevention of Corruption Act) — accused must prove certain facts
Prosecution fails to discharge initial burden — accused need not even present defense (no case to answer)
Prosecution creates reasonable doubt by its own evidence — acquittal follows without defense evidence
CONCEPT_BURDEN_OF_PROOF.typical_edge_pattern: (Prosecution_Alleges_Offense) --[must prove beyond reasonable doubt]--> (Burden_on_Prosecution_IEA101/BSA104) --[unless statutory presumption shifts burden]--> (Burden_Shifts_to_Accused)
CONCEPT_BURDEN_OF_PROOF.key_phrases: "burden of proof", "Section 101", "Section 106", "BSA 104", "BSA 109", "beyond reasonable doubt", "preponderance of probability", "onus of proof", "who asserts must prove", "especially within the knowledge of", "reverse burden"
CONCEPT_BURDEN_OF_PROOF.establishing_cases: Woolmington v. DPP (1935 — adopted in Indian law), Shambhu Nath Mehra v. State of Ajmer (1956), Rang Bahadur Singh v. State of UP (2000), Dataram Singh v. State of UP (2018)

CONCEPT_COURT_PRESUMPTION.label: Court Presumption
requires: [AND — under IEA 114 / BSA 119]

The court may presume the existence of any fact which it thinks likely to have happened, regard being had to: the common course of natural events, human conduct, and public and private business, in their relation to the facts of the particular case
The presumption is discretionary ('may presume'), not mandatory ('shall presume')

The illustrations to IEA 114 / BSA 119 provide specific examples of presumable facts (e.g., stolen property found with a person soon after theft, man in possession of stolen property recently after theft is either thief or receiver)
defeaters: Presumption is rebuttable — accused can lead evidence to show that the presumed fact does not exist
The connection between the base fact and the presumed fact is too remote or speculative
Presumption cannot replace proof — it supplements evidence, does not substitute for it
Other evidence contradicts the presumption
The illustration under IEA 114 / BSA 119 is merely indicative, not exhaustive — court must independently evaluate whether presumption is warranted in the specific case
CONCEPT_COURT_PRESUMPTION.key_phrases: "court may presume", "Section 114", "BSA 119", "natural presumption", "common course of natural events", "human conduct", "stolen property recently in possession", "presumption of fact", "rebuttable presumption", "illustration (a)"
CONCEPT_COURT_PRESUMPTION.establishing_cases: Earabhadrappa v. State of Karnataka (1983), Trimbak Bapu Godbole v. Dattaji Pandurang (1890), Sanatan Naskar v. State of WB (2010), State of Rajasthan v. Kashi Ram (2006)

deferred_application.resolution_logic: Search FORWARD for resumption markers ("Reverting to...", "Coming now to...", "We now address...") or topic-matching paragraphs
deferred_application.graph_implication: Creates DEFERRED_EDGE; marks node with "awaits_resolution" property

noted_above.resolution_logic: Search BACKWARD for paragraphs containing explicit legal holdings, factual findings, or referenced precedent; semantic similarity + entity matching
noted_above.graph_implication: Creates BACK_REFERENCE edge; current reasoning DEPENDS on earlier established facts/law

aforesaid_facts.resolution_logic: Resolve to Facts section or most recent factual narrative; "aforesaid" = immediately preceding material
aforesaid_facts.graph_implication: Creates FACTUAL_DEPENDENCY edge; holdings CONDITIONAL on factual findings

Having so held... we proceed to consider
sequential_doctrine.example: "Having held that Article 21 is violated, the question of proportionality assumes importance"
sequential_doctrine.resolution_logic: Doctrine A → Doctrine B dependency; track "having held" to identify primary doctrine
sequential_doctrine.graph_implication: Creates DOCTRINAL_CHAIN edge (transitive support); CRITICAL - if Doctrine A fails, chain collapses

signals: The trinity exists between Article 14, Article 19 and Article 21
golden_triangle_interconnection.example: "A seven-Judge Bench held that a trinity exists between Article 14, Article 19 and Article 21. All these articles have to be read together" - Maneka Gandhi
golden_triangle_interconnection.resolution_logic: Multiple provisions form interconnected doctrine; violation of one triggers all
golden_triangle_interconnection.graph_implication: Creates CONJUNCTIVE_DEPENDENCY edges; mutually reinforcing doctrinal base

hypothetical_concession.graph_implication: Creates HYPOTHETICAL_CONCESSION edge; ALTERNATIVE_REASONING - holding STRENGTHENED not dependent on this path

but_for_causation.graph_implication: Creates DETERMINATIVE_FACTOR edge; CRITICAL causal link

single_factor_determinism.graph_implication: Creates DETERMINATIVE edge marked as SUFFICIENT; other edges marked as SURPLUS_TO_REQUIREMENTS - **HIGH VALUE for identifying CRITICAL edges**
